title: Moroccan Fintech Platform Foundation
subtitle: Designing shared product infrastructure for finance tools built around Moroccan business constraints
description: A systems case study on the shared architecture behind multiple finance products, focused on compliance, tenancy, auditability, and reusable workflow primitives.

images.alt: Platform concept artifact showing a fintech product surface
images.caption: Concept artifact for shared fintech product surfaces.

images.alt: Dashboard concept artifact for operational finance workflows
images.caption: Dashboard artifact used to explore operational density and review workflows.

sections.title: Why a platform layer mattered
paragraphs: Generafi and Synapcity look like separate products, but they share deeper questions: how to model trust, compliance, permissions, and operational context for Moroccan businesses.

The platform foundation is a way to avoid rebuilding those decisions from scratch. It defines the shared primitives that finance-focused products can rely on while still allowing each product to express its own workflow.

pullQuote.text: The platform exists to make local constraints reusable instead of rediscovered in every product.

sections.title: The questions we had to answer

decisions.title: Which primitives should be shared across products?
decisions.teaser: Too much platform work slows product learning; too little creates repeated architecture debt.
decisions.answer: The shared layer focuses on tenant identity, permissions, audit events, document metadata, and workflow status. Product-specific logic stays in each product until it repeats enough to justify extraction.
alternatives: Create a full internal platform before validating products
Share only UI components and keep data models separate

decisions.title: How should compliance be represented across product lines?
decisions.teaser: Compliance cannot be a PDF attached at the end of the workflow.
decisions.answer: Compliance is modeled as structured events, rule references, approvals, and evidence links that can be reused by reporting and audit features.
alternatives: Store compliance evidence as file attachments
Leave compliance modeling to each product
Use generic notes fields for audit context

decisions.title: How much should the system optimize for low-operational overhead?
decisions.teaser: Emerging-market products need cost discipline from the beginning.
decisions.answer: The foundation favors shared infrastructure, explicit tenant boundaries, and boring operational primitives. The goal is to keep the system understandable before adding distributed complexity.
alternatives: Separate infrastructure per product and tenant
Outsource critical primitives to unrelated tools

callout.text: The reusable layer should encode constraints that are stable across products, not guesses about future features.

designDecision.title: Shared Trust Primitives
designDecision.description: Tenant identity, permission checks, audit events, and evidence links are treated as shared primitives used by each product.
designDecision.why: These concerns appear in every finance workflow and become expensive to retrofit later.

constraints.title: Product learning is still underway
constraints.description: The platform cannot assume every future workflow before the products have been validated.

constraints.title: Local compliance is specific
constraints.description: Moroccan finance workflows have regulatory and operational details that generic SaaS primitives often miss.

constraints.title: Operational cost matters
constraints.description: The architecture has to support small customers without requiring enterprise-level infrastructure spend.

sections.title: What we chose not to do

tradeoffs.title: No broad internal platform upfront
tradeoffs.whatWeChose: Extract only the primitives that already repeat across products.
tradeoffs.whatItCost: Some duplication remains while product workflows are still moving.
tradeoffs.whenWeReconsider: When multiple products independently need the same workflow or domain service.

tradeoffs.title: No heavy distributed architecture by default
tradeoffs.whatWeChose: Prefer clear module boundaries and shared infrastructure before introducing separate services.
tradeoffs.whatItCost: Some scaling paths may require later extraction.
tradeoffs.whenWeReconsider: When usage patterns create independent scaling or reliability requirements.

milestones.title: Shared constraint mapping
milestones.description: Compared Generafi and Synapcity workflows to identify repeated trust, compliance, and permission needs.

milestones.title: Primitive definition
milestones.description: Defined tenant, audit, evidence, and workflow-status primitives as the first shared layer.

milestones.title: Product feedback loop
milestones.description: Kept product-specific behavior local until repeated needs made a shared abstraction worthwhile.

metrics.label: Shared primitives
metrics.description: Tenant identity, permissions, audit events, evidence links, and workflow status.

metrics.label: Product lines
metrics.description: Generafi and Synapcity supplied the initial platform constraints.

metrics.label: Compliance posture
metrics.description: A shared approach to evidence, approvals, and audit events.

metrics.label: Extraction rule
metrics.description: A primitive is extracted only after it appears across more than one product workflow.

reflections.insight: A platform is useful when it preserves product speed. If it predicts too much, it becomes another source of drag.
reflections.transferable: Extract shared infrastructure from repeated constraints, not from abstract architectural preference.

reflections.insight: Local market constraints can be a design advantage when they are modeled deliberately instead of patched around.
reflections.transferable: For emerging-market software, encode local operating realities in the domain model early.

sections.title: The work ahead
sections.intro: The next phase is to keep tightening the shared layer while product workflows become clearer.

items.title: Audit event schema
items.description: Stabilize a shared event format that works across payroll, documents, approvals, and knowledge artifacts.

items.title: Permission capability map
items.description: Define capabilities that can be reused across finance and collaboration workflows.

items.title: Product extraction criteria
items.description: Document when a repeated behavior should move from a product module into the platform layer.

problem: Moroccan finance products repeatedly need the same tenancy, audit, evidence, permission, and workflow primitives.
solution: Extract shared primitives only after repeated product needs prove they belong in the platform layer.
role: Platform strategy, shared domain modeling, and product architecture across finance workflows.
proof: The case study identifies tenant identity, permissions, audit events, evidence links, and workflow status as the first shared primitives.
impact: The platform foundation reduces repeated compliance work while preserving product speed for Generafi, Synapcity, and future finance tools.
challenges: Deciding which compliance concerns are genuinely shared
Keeping local market constraints visible in reusable infrastructure
learnings: A platform is useful when it preserves product speed.
Local market constraints can become reusable product infrastructure when modeled deliberately.
